safety_message__plan.safety_message_expanded_safety_message_s:
GENERAL OPERATIONAL SAFETY MESSAGE – REDWOOD CREEK WILDFIRE, OPERATIONAL PERIOD 06:00–18:00, 2025-08-14

1. INCIDENT OVERVIEW AND KEY HAZARDS
- This is an active wildland-urban interface (WUI) fire burning in mixed conifer and oak woodland with steep terrain, loose rock, and heavy dead-and-down fuels.
- Forecasted temperatures: 92–98°F with low relative humidity (12–18%). Afternoon upslope winds 10–18 mph with gusts up to 25 mph, especially along ridgelines and in canyons.
- Primary hazards:
  • Rapid fire spread and shifting wind conditions, especially after 1400 hours.
  • Rolling rocks and logs on steep slopes; potential for sprains, fractures, and head injuries.
  • Heat-related illness (heat exhaustion, heat stroke) and dehydration.
  • Smoke exposure causing eye and respiratory irritation; reduced visibility for driving and line work.
  • Snags (dead or fire-weakened trees) and falling branches, especially in previously burned areas and along dozer lines.
  • Fatigue from extended shifts and multiple operational periods.
  • Vehicle collisions on narrow access roads and at congested ICP and staging areas.

2. LCES (LOOKOUTS, COMMUNICATIONS, ESCAPE ROUTES, SAFETY ZONES)
- Lookouts:
  • Division Supervisors will designate lookouts for each division and ensure they are clearly identified on the division briefing.
  • Lookouts must have reliable communications, clear vantage points, and must not be assigned other distracting tasks.
- Communications:
  • Use the IAP Communications Plan (ICS 205) for all primary, alternate, contingency, and emergency frequencies.
  • Conduct radio checks at the start of shift and whenever entering a new division.
  • Use clear text; avoid codes. Immediately report changes in fire behavior, spot fires, near misses, or injuries.
- Escape Routes:
  • Identify at least two escape routes from your work location at all times. Communicate them to your crew and reassess frequently as fire behavior and operations change.
  • Mark escape routes with flagging where appropriate; avoid routes that cross unburned heavy fuels during periods of active fire behavior.
- Safety Zones:
  • Safety zones must be identified in the division briefing and re-evaluated by company officers and crew leaders throughout the day.
  • Ensure safety zones are large enough for your resources given current and forecasted fire behavior. Do not rely solely on vehicles as safety zones.

3. PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE)
- Required PPE for line personnel:
  • Fire-resistant (Nomex) shirt and pants, properly worn and buttoned.
  • NFPA-approved wildland fire boots (8" minimum height), broken-in and in good repair.
  • Hard hat with chin strap; keep chin strap fastened on steep terrain and during active felling or dozer operations.
  • Leather gloves; carry a spare pair if available.
  • Eye protection (goggles or safety glasses) and hearing protection when around chainsaws, pumps, or aircraft.
  • Fire shelter must be carried at all times; review fire shelter deployment procedures at the start of shift.
- Medical and personal considerations:
  • Report any medical conditions (e.g., asthma, severe allergies, diabetes) to your supervisor in confidence so that appropriate precautions can be taken.
  • If you are taking prescription medications that could affect heat tolerance, alert your supervisor and monitor yourself closely for early signs of heat stress.

4. HEAT, HYDRATION, AND FATIGUE MANAGEMENT
- Hydration:
  • Start hydrating before your shift. Aim for at least 1 quart of water per hour during heavy work; alternate water with electrolyte drinks.
  • Avoid excessive caffeine and completely avoid energy drinks; these can mask fatigue and contribute to dehydration and cardiac stress.
- Heat Illness Prevention:
  • Use the buddy system; watch each other for early signs of heat exhaustion (headache, dizziness, nausea, irritability, heavy sweating, muscle cramps).
  • At the first signs of heat stress, move the person to shade, loosen clothing, provide cool water in small sips, and notify your supervisor. Do not wait until symptoms are severe.
  • For suspected heat stroke (confusion, altered mental status, hot/dry skin or profuse sweating, collapse): this is a medical emergency. Call it in immediately as a priority medical incident, begin active cooling (cool packs to neck, armpits, groin, soak clothing), and prepare for rapid transport.
- Fatigue:
  • Extended operations increase risk of errors, near misses, and injuries. Be honest about your fatigue level.
  • Supervisors: rotate high-exertion tasks, build in short rest breaks in the shade, and encourage your crews to speak up when they are at their limit.

5. DRIVING AND VEHICLE SAFETY
- General:
  • Seatbelts are mandatory at all times, in all vehicles, no exceptions.
  • Obey posted speed limits and adjust speed for dust, smoke, narrow roads, and pedestrian traffic in and around ICP, staging, and helibases.
  • No texting, emailing, or cell phone use while driving. If you must use a phone or data device, pull safely off the roadway and stop first.
- Apparatus and Heavy Equipment:
  • Conduct a walk-around inspection before moving engines, tenders, and heavy equipment. Check for personnel, hose, and equipment around and under vehicles.
  • Use ground guides when backing or maneuvering in tight or congested areas.
  • Maintain at least 20 feet of separation between vehicles on dirt roads to reduce collision risk and dust impacts.

6. SNAGS, FALLING OBJECTS, AND SAW OPERATIONS
- Snag Hazards:
  • Identify and flag hazard trees and snags during line scouting and construction. Avoid working under snags whenever possible.
  • Do not rest, stage equipment, or hold briefings under dead or fire-weakened trees.
- Chainsaw and Felling Safety:
  • Only qualified sawyers with appropriate PPE and current certification are to perform felling operations.
  • Maintain proper escape routes and clear work areas before felling.
  • All personnel must stay outside of the established hazard radius; sawyers and lookouts are responsible for enforcing this.

7. MEDICAL PLAN AND EMERGENCY PROCEDURES
- Follow the Medical Plan (ICS 206) for all injuries and illnesses.
- Immediately report:
  • Any injury requiring more than simple on-site first aid.
  • All near misses, especially those involving entrapment potential, vehicle movement, or falling objects.
- When reporting an emergency over the radio:
  • Begin with: "Emergency, Emergency, Emergency" and identify your location (division, landmark, GPS if available).
  • Provide nature of injury/illness, number of patients, immediate hazards, and access route for EMS.
- Ensure that every crew member knows:
  • Their division identifier.
  • Designated medical transport options.
  • Closest helispot or ambulance access point.

8. COVID/RESPIRATORY ILLNESS AND GENERAL HYGIENE
- While COVID-19 is currently at low community transmission levels in this area, respiratory illnesses continue to impact responder availability.
- Practices:
  • Do not report for duty if you have fever, severe cough, or other acute respiratory symptoms. Notify your supervisor and medical unit.
  • Practice frequent hand hygiene (soap and water or alcohol-based sanitizer), especially before eating and after using restrooms or handling shared tools.
  • Cough or sneeze into your elbow and avoid sharing utensils, drink containers, or cigarettes.

9. CAMP AND BASE SAFETY
- Slips, trips, and falls are common off-line injuries.
  • Use headlamps at night; watch for guide wires, hoses, and uneven ground.
  • Report any poorly lit or high-traffic trip hazards to Base and Facilities.
- Food and Hydration:
  • Eat regular meals; do not skip breakfast even if you are not very hungry. Caloric intake is essential for heat tolerance and sustained performance.
- Personal Conduct:
  • Alcohol and recreational drugs are strictly prohibited during assignment.
  • Harassment, discrimination, and bullying will not be tolerated. Report any concerns through your chain of command or directly to Safety.

10. MENTAL HEALTH, STRESS, AND FAMILY COMMUNICATION
- Wildfire operations are stressful and can be emotionally taxing, particularly for those with family or property in threatened areas.
- You are encouraged to:
  • Check in regularly with family when off shift to reduce anxiety on both sides.
  • Use available peer support or Employee Assistance Program resources if you experience difficulty sleeping, persistent anxiety, or intrusive thoughts related to the incident.
  • Talk to your supervisor if stress is affecting your concentration or decision-making on the line.

11. KEY EXPECTATIONS FOR THIS OPERATIONAL PERIOD
- Anchor, flank, and hold lines; do not get ahead of your secure anchor points.
- Maintain constant situational awareness of wind shifts, spotting, and changes in fuel conditions.
- Speak up if a tactic or assignment feels unsafe; use the "Turn Down" protocol when appropriate and work with your supervisor to find safer alternatives.
- Everyone, regardless of rank, has the authority and responsibility to pause operations if they observe an immediate life-safety hazard.

REMEMBER: NO STRUCTURE OR PIECE OF LAND IS WORTH A LIFE. IF CONDITIONS BECOME UNSAFE, DISENGAGE, REASSESS, AND REDEPLOY USING LCES.
safety_message__plan.approved_site_safety_plans_located_at: Approved site safety plans are available in hard copy at the Incident Command Post (ICP) Planning Section tent, on the information board adjacent to the main briefing area, and digitally via the incident’s shared drive under “Safety/Site Safety Plans/Redwood Creek Wildfire – OP 2025-08-14.”
